mnemonics.title: Remember Constraint Types
mnemonics.content: P-F-U-N-C-D: Primary, Foreign, Unique, Not null, Check, Default - Six constraints to protect your data!

mnemonics.title: Remember Data Types Priority
mnemonics.content: NVCDT: Numbers (INT, FLOAT), Varchar, Char, Date, Text - Common SQL data types order

mnemonics.title: Remember ALTER uses
mnemonics.content: A-D-M-C: Add column, Drop column, Modify column - ALTER TABLE can do all this!

acronyms.usage: SQL commands that define database structure: CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE
Standard language for managing relational databases
Unique identifier for each row in a table, cannot be NULL
Column that references PRIMARY KEY of another table
Represents missing or unknown data - not zero or empty string
Variable-length string data type, stores actual length of data
Fixed-length string data type, pads with spaces
Automatically generates sequential numbers (called AUTO_INCREMENT in MySQL)

visualTips.visualization: Think of a table as a spreadsheet: columns are headers (names, data types), rows are data entries. Constraints are rules written below headers.
Imagine parent-child relationship: Parent table (with PRIMARY KEY) holds master data. Child table (with FOREIGN KEY) references parent. Like a family tree.
Think of constraints as bouncers at a club: NOT NULL checks if you exist, UNIQUE checks if you're unique, CHECK validates your ID, PRIMARY KEY gives you VIP status.
CHAR is like a fixed parking space (always takes full size). VARCHAR is like valet parking (takes only the space your car needs).

analogies.analogy: Like Aadhaar/Social Security number - unique identifier for each citizen that no one else can have.
Like a library book card with borrower ID - links the book record to the member record.
Like mandatory fields in a form - you cannot submit without filling them.
Like auto-fill in forms - if you don't enter anything, the system uses the preset value.
Like age restrictions for certain content - validates that values meet specific criteria.
Like email addresses in a system - each must be different, but you can have multiple accounts.
Like designing a form template before collecting actual filled forms.
